[JS Weird World] Bonus: Closures, One More Time
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript

I already covered closures earlier in this series, but honestly I still felt half-sure about them. So I pulled the closure topic out into its own bonus episode to dig in a bit more. This post walks through what a closure is, why you'd want one, and a money-tracking example that shows why inner variables stick around instead of getting wiped out.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-65-object-and-its-prototype/
[JS Weird World] No.65 Our Object and Its Prototype

Now we're getting ready to set up the Greeter object so we can make it behave as close to jQuery as possible. It takes a little trick, though. In this post we wire up the constructor, hook up the prototype chain, and attach the whole thing to the global scope so you can call it with G$().
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/23/js-weird-world-55-arrays-and-for-in/
[JS Weird World] No.55 A Dangerous Reminder: Arrays and for in

We've already seen some of the dangerous corners of the built-in function constructors. But arrays and for in have their own traps too. In this post I'll show why looping an array with for in can bite you, and why a plain for loop is the safer bet.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/21/jsweirdworld-47-understanding-prototypes/
[JS Weird World] No.47 Understanding Prototypes

What exactly is a prototype in JavaScript? How do __proto__ and the prototype chain actually work? This post starts from how the dot operator looks things up, and walks you through how the prototype chain links objects together one by one.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/20/jsweirdworld-41-closures-and-callbacks/
[JS Weird World] No.41 Closures and Callbacks

We've already covered closures, first-class functions, and the rest — and honestly, just like the course instructor warned, my brain is close to melting. So this chapter just builds on the previous one. The thing is, if you've ever used setTimeout or jQuery in JavaScript, you've already been using closures without realising it. So let's talk about closures and callbacks.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/19/js-weird-world-34-automatic-semicolon-insertion/
[JS Weird World] No.34 A Dangerous Little Reminder: Automatic Semicolon Insertion

Time for the first real "danger ahead" reminder in this series: automatic semicolon insertion. We already know no programming language is truly perfect, and we've covered the gotchas in JavaScript. But this one is genuinely dangerous — it's super common, super easy to trip over, and a pain to track down. So let's make sure we avoid it.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/19/jsweirdworld-30-arrays-hold-anything/
[JS Weird World] No.30 Quick Reminder: An Array Can Hold Literally Anything

In a lot of languages an array can only hold one type of thing. JavaScript doesn't care — you can mix numbers, strings, booleans, objects, even functions in the same array. Here's a quick reminder of why that works, plus how to call a function that's sitting inside an array (and pass it another array element as an argument).
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/18/js-weird-world-27-function-statements-vs-expressions/
[JS Weird World] No.27 Function Statements vs Function Expressions

Last chapter we figured out that functions are just objects. Now we're going to use that idea to do some genuinely fun and powerful stuff. But before we get there, we need to understand the difference between function statements and function expressions — and why one gets hoisted and the other doesn't.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/17/js-weird-world-20-default-values/
[JS Weird World] No.20 Default Values

Last time we dug into some of the quirks of coercion. This time we're going to flip it around and put those quirks to work — starting with default values. What happens when you call a function without passing the argument it expects? And how do you set a default so it doesn't blow up? Let's find out.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/jsweirdworld-17-coercion/
[JS Weird World] No.17 A Quick Reminder: Type Coercion

What does coercion actually mean? It's when JavaScript quietly turns one type into another — like a String into a Boolean. It happens all over the place in JS, and in this post we'll look at a simple example of how it shows up when you do math on two different types.
